THE COURT ORDERED THE FOLLOWING AMENDED JUDGMENT ENTERED: IT IS ADJUDGED THAT PLAINTIFF THOMPSON, THEA CHRISTINA TAKE NOTHING FROM DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ON THE COMPLAINT, SAID DEFENDANT TO HAVE JUDGMENT THEREON FOR $17,114.17 COSTS, TOTAL JUDGMENT $17,114.17, JUDGEMENT IN FAVOR OF DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO AGAINST PLAINTIFF THEA CHRISTINA THOMPSON, SAID PLAINTIFF TO TAKE NOTHING FROM DEFENDANT. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO IS ENTITLED TO RECOVERY OF STATUTORY COSTS, FEES, AND DISBURSEMENTS IN THE AMOUNT OF $17,114.17. PLAINTIFF WAIVES HER RIGHT TO POST-TRIAL MOTIONS AND APPEAL. (SEE SCANNED DOCUMENT)

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE OCT-14-2022 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ON THE CAUSE OF ACTION FOR DANGEROUS CONDITION OF PUBLIC PROPERTY (GOV'T 835) IS DENIED. DEFENDANT'S ALTERNATIVE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION IS DENIED AS MOOT. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS.) ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: VEDICA PURI; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/VP)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-14-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion for Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion for summary judgment and motion for summary adjudication on the cause of action for dangerous condition of public property (Gov't 835) is denied. Defendant fails to maintain its burden of production. (See CCP 437c(p)(2).)

"The papers are to be construed strictly against the moving party and liberally in favor of the opposing party; any doubts regarding the propriety of summary judgment are to be resolved in favor of the opposing party." (Kulesa v. Castleberry (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 103, 112.)

"The existence of a dangerous condition ordinarily is a question of fact, but the issue may be resolved as a matter of law if reasonable minds can come to only one conclusion." (Zelig v. County of Los Angeles (2002) 27 Cal.4th 1112, 1133.) Defendant fails to demonstrate as a matter of law that the alleged defect was trivial. (See Green Decl. Ex. A; McCullaugh Decl., Ex. A) In determining whether an alleged defect is trivial, the court does not adopt a "tape measure test" but must take account of all surrounding circumstances. (See Aitkenhead v. City of County of San Francisco (1957) 150 Cal.App.2d 49, 51; Ursino v. Big Boy Restaurants (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 394, 397; Kasparian v. AvalonBay Communities (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 11, 27.) Here, a reasonable trier of fact could conclude that the sidewalk constituted a dangerous condition per CACI 1003 [unsafe conditions]. The court further notes that a condition may be more dangerous when one travels in one direction (e.g. parallel to the defect) rather than another. As Mr. Wong aptly notes, the path that one takes can make the defect more or less visible. (Wong Decl., par. 6.)

Defendant's alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as moot. Plaintiff concedes that she is not pleading a negligence/premises liability claim against defendant, a public entity.

Order signed.

Judge: Vedica Puri; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/VP)
